name: Wells' Criteria for Pulmonary Embolism
description: Objectifies risk of pulmonary embolism and provides an estimated pre-test probability.
purpose: Risk stratify patients for pulmonary embolism (PE) and guide further diagnostic workup (d-dimer or CT angiogram).
when_to_use: Patients with clinical suspicion for pulmonary embolism
Determining pre-test probability of PE to guide further testing
when_not_to_use: Should not be applied to all patients with chest pain or shortness of breath without clinical suspicion for PE
Should not be used before history and physical exam

items.label: dvt
items.description: Clinical signs and symptoms of DVT

items.label: pe_number_one_diagnosis
items.description: PE is the most or equally likely diagnosis

items.label: immobilization_or_surgery
items.description: Immobilization at least 3 days or surgery in the previous 4 weeks

items.label: previous_pe_dvt
items.description: Previous, objectively diagnosed PE or DVT

items.label: malignancy
items.description: Malignancy with treatment within 6 months or palliative

formula: dvt + pe_number_one_diagnosis + heart_rate_100 + immobilization_or_surgery + previous_pe_dvt + hemoptysis + malignancy

interpretation.description: Three-Tier Model: Low Risk (1.3% incidence of PE). Consider d-dimer testing to rule out PE. If negative, consider stopping workup. If positive, consider CTA.
Three-Tier Model: Moderate Risk (16.2% incidence of PE). Consider high sensitivity d-dimer testing or CTA. If dimer negative, consider stopping workup. If positive, consider CTA.
Three-Tier Model: High Risk (37.5% incidence of PE). Consider CTA. D-dimer testing is not recommended.
Two-Tier Model: PE Unlikely (12.1% incidence of PE). Consider high sensitivity d-dimer testing. If negative, consider stopping workup. If positive, consider CTA.
Two-Tier Model: PE Likely (37.1% incidence of PE). Consider CTA testing.

references.citation: Wells PS, Anderson DR, Rodger M, Stiell I, Dreyer JF, Barnes D, Forgie M, Kovacs G, Ward J, Kovacs MJ. Excluding pulmonary embolism at the bedside without diagnostic imaging: management of patients with suspected pulmonary embolism presenting to the emergency department by using a simple clinical model and d-dimer. Ann Intern Med. 2001 Jul 17;135(2):98-107

notes: Wells' can be used with either 3 tiers (low, moderate, high) or 2 tiers (unlikely, likely). ACEP's 2011 clinical policy on PE supports the two-tier model. The model should be applied only after history and physical exam suggests that venous thromboembolism is a diagnostic possibility.
